6. Fields

Definition 37. A subfield is a field under the same addition and multiplication operations as . Equivalently, is a unital (shares multiplicative identity) subring of that is closed under taking inverses of nonzero elements
Proposition 40. Any subfield of contains .
Proof

A subfield of inherits the zero, identity, and operations of . Thus any subfield contains and thus .

□

The three most important classes of fields are

  1. Number fields (subfield of ).
  2. Finite fields
  3. Function fields

6.1. Algebraic, transcendental elements, extension fields

Definition 38. Let and be fields. When , say is an extension field of . forms a vector space over , and we define the of over as .

Examples.

  1. for square free is an extension field with
  2. .
  3. . A basis is .
  4. .

How can we construct an extension field of ? Take irreducible , and let where is the image of in . For example, . Note that .

Notation: is the smallest ring containing and . is the smallest field containing and . The first consists of polynomials in with ratoinal coefficients while the latter consists of rational functions. They are equal if and only if is algebraic (Proposition 41)

Definition 39. Let . We say is algebraic if it is a root of an irreducible monic polynomial with coefficients in . Otherwise, is transcendental over .

This collapses to our familiar definition of algebraic numbers when and .

Proposition 41.

  1. is algebraic if and only if is finite.
  2. is algebraic if and only if , i.e. is a field.
  3. If is transcendental over , then as a ring.
Proof
  1. If is algebraic, then its degree over equals the degree of its irreducible polynomial over . If is finite, then the powers have a linear independence relation.
  2. Since is a PID, we have is irreducible if and only if is maximal, if and only if is a field.
  3. If is transcendental, the surjective evaluation map is injective, and hence is an isomorphism.

□

If is algebraic over with irreducible polynomial , then where is is a basis for as a vector space over . Thus a necessary condition for two algebraic elements to generate isomorphic field extensions is that they have the same degree, since all bases of a vector space have the same length. This is clearly not sufficient, as all quadratic number fields have degree over .

Here’s a cool case where . Let be a complex root of and be a root of . This is hard to see if we were only given the polynomials.

We will focus -isomorphisms: isomorphisms that fix and send to . The next proposition will gives a clear condition.

Proposition 42 (Artin 15.2.8 — Conditions for F-isomorphism). Let be a field, and be two field extensions, and . Suppose are algebraic over . There exists an -isomorphism mapping if and only if and have the same irreducible polynomials over .
Proof

Consider the isomorphisms and granted by between and respectively. is the required isomorphism . Conversely, if such isomorphism exists then preserves roots so they must have the same irreducible polynomial.

□

The isomorphism is not unique. Consider .

Definition 40.

  1. A field homomorphism preserves addition and multiplication. Preserving additive and multiplicative identities is implied.
  2. Let be two extension fields of . A homomorphism is an -homomorphism if is the identity map on F.

An example of a field homomorphism is conjugation. The lemma implies that for a polynomial with real coefficients, is a root implies is a root.

Lemma 31 (F-homomorphism preserves roots). Let be an -homomorphism. Let . If is a root of , then so is .

Corollary. -automorphisms preserve algebraic relations.

For example, in , we can only map to (root of its own minimal polynomial) and not , since satisfies

Proof

Let be a basis of over , and let be a basis of over . Then I claim elements in the form form a basis of over Spanning and linear independence properties are both obvious.

□

Corollary. Let with . Then,

  1. Any is algebraic over .

6.2. Finding the irreducible polynomials

Suppose is algebraic over . How can you find an irreducible polynomial of ?

  • Method 1. Bash a linear dependence relation between powers of .
  • Method 2. Use the fact that any -automorphism maps roots of to roots of and guess what the automorphism might look likei. Suppose . Then the possible roots are . Multiple the product of these terms to get .

This example shows that , which doesn’t seem obvious immediately. We have and then we can extract . This is a preview of the primitive element theorem.

6.3. Ruler and Compass Constructions

See cool examples in the textbook that build on each other.

Proposition 43 (Lines and Circles).

  1. Lines and circles between points in subfield of the field of real numbers are defined by linear and quadratic equations with coefficients in .
  2. The points of intersections of lines and circles have coordinates in , or a real quadratic field extension of .
Proof
  1. Obvious
  2. The intersection of a line and a circle is a quadratic equation. The intersection between two circles is also a quadratic equation (two circles intersectin at most two points). In general two conics may intersect in four points, but two circles intersect in at most two points. This is because we can subtract the two equations and get a linear equation to eliminate one variable, yielding just a quadratic.

□

First, begin by assuming the rationals are constructible. We will prove this at the end of the section.

Theorem 29. Let be a constructible point. For some integer there is a chain of fields

(43)

such that

  • is a subfield of the real numbers.
  • The coordinates of are in .
  • For each , the degree is equal to .

Hence the degree is a power of .

Proof

Follows from multiplicative property of degree and Proposition 43.

□

The converse is not true, i.e. there exists real numbers of degree over that aren’t constructible (Exercise 16.9.17).

Say a real number is constructible if the point is constructible starting from and . By Theorem 29, then is an algebraic number and its degree over is a power of , i.e. there there is a polynomial of integer coefficients of degree 2 that kills .

To prove the impossibility of certain geometric constructions, show that if a certain construction were possible, then it would also be possible to construct an algebraic number whos degree over is not a power of . For example, we can drop an altitude.

Example 12. It is easy to construct an angle of 60°. Show that you cannot trisect it, and thus there is no general method of trisection.

The reason is that has irreducible polynomial of degree so it is not constructible.

Example 13. The regular -gon cannot be constructed. More generally, if is a prime and the regular -gon can be constructed with rule and compass, then for some integer .
Proof

Consider the irreducible polynomial is .

□

Gauss proved the converse and constructed the regular 17-gon.

Theorem 30. In the ascending chain above, every element of is constructible.
Proof

Use induction on . Base case. Every rational is constructible. Follows from the next lemma and the fact that all integers are trivially constructible. Inductive step. Since any extension of degree can be obtained by adjoining a square root of a number in ( is constructive by induction hypothesis), it suffices to prove the following lemma.

□

Lemma 32 (Artin 15.5.11).

  1. The constructible numbers form a subfield of , i.e. if and are constructible, then so are .
  2. If is a positive constructible number, then so is .
Proof

  1. Given a triangle and one side of a second triangle, the second triangle can be constructed by parallels (see earlier constructions in the textbook). Use similar triangles with and .

  2. Draw the altitude eof a right triangle inscribed in a circle of diameter .

□

6.4. Adjoining roots

Let be an irreducible polynomial of . We can construct an extension . Let be the image of . Then is a root of . Irreduciblity is required so that is maximal and is a field.

This only adjoins one root at a time (think of first isomorphism theorem). For with distinct irrational roots, for all three . We study this more in Galois theory, like the degree of over .

If is reducible then we can use CRT (Theorem 12) to write as a direct product of fields. For example,

(44)

Lemma 33 (Finite field is cyclic).

  1. A polynomial of degree has at most -roots in .
  2. Let be a finite subgroup of . Then is a cyclic group.
  3. Let be a finite field. Then, is a cyclic group.
Proof
  1. Obvious.
  2. Apply the structure theorem to get where Consider the roots of the polynomial where . The number of roots equals since each is some multiple of already. and we have choices for each component. By (1) we must have .
  3. Corollary of (2).

□

Definition 41. A polynomial splits completely in an extension field if it factors into linear factors in . We also say splits .
Proposition 44 (Existence of field extension that splits ). Let be a field and . Then there exists a field extension of such that splits completely.
Proof

Induct on the degree of . If is irreducible, then is an extension field where has a root and can be expressed as with . Otherwise, separately extend on the irreducible factors of .

□

Proposition 45. Let be irreducible.

  1. is a double root of if and only if .
  2. has no multiple root in any field extension unless =0
  3. If the characteristic of is zero, has no multiple root in any extension field of .
Proof
  1. Consider polynomial division.
  2. Suppose . Since , if has a multiple root, then and share a common factor with . Let . We must have or but since we can only have the first possibility, and they cannot share a common factor. Thus we get a contradiction.
  3. Corollary of (2): is nonconstant in a field of characteristic zero, so its derivative cannot be zero.

□

For example, let be the fraction field . Let . and . Can be seen from binomial theorem.

6.5. Finite Fields

For any field , there is a ring homomorphism given by . Its kernel is the ideal

Proposition 46 (Finite fields have prime power order).

  1. Let be a finite field. Then the characteristic of is for some prime .
  2. The order of is a power of .
Proof

We proved (1) in 18.701. Recall it went like: Suppose for . Then and are both nonzero, and . But a field has no zero divisors, contradiction.

Let’s prove (1,2) using defined above. We have . is a subring of , so it is an integral domain implying that is a prime ideal of , implying is a field, meaning it is a subfield of . Hence is an extension field of and can be viewed as a vector space over , which has elements. Hence has order that is a prime power of .

□

Notation. We will write for a finite field of order . We say has characteristic . As an additive group, it isomorphic to but not necessarily as a ring/field.

Example 14 (Quadratic extension of ). is the only irreducible quadratic polynomial. We have . is the Klein four group.

Lemma 34. Let and be a finite field of elements.

  1. has no multiple root in any field extension of .
  2. In .
  3. The elements of are the roots of the polynomial , i.e. splits .
Proof
  1. The derivative is nonzero, so apply Proposition 45.
  2. Obvious from binomial theorem
  3. There are roots of the polynomial and elements of , so it suffices to show each element of is a root of . Apply Lemma 33 so that every element satisfies .

□

Theorem 31 (All fields of order q are isomorphic). There exists a unique (up to isomorphism) field of order .

Definition 42. Let denote the field of elements.

Theorem 32 (Artin 15.7.3 — Number of irreducible polynomials in ). Let be a prime integer and be a positive power of . Let be a field of order

  1. The elements of are the roots of the polynomial .
  2. The irreducible factors of over are the irreducible polynomials with .
  3. The multiplicative group of nonzero elements of is a cyclic group of order .
  4. There exists a field of order , and all fields of order are isomorphic.
  5. A field of order contains a subfield of order if and only if .
  6. For every , there exists an irreducible polynomial in of degree .

Example 16. How many degree irreducible polynomials in are there? What about degree 10?

Use (1) from the theorem above. Let be the number of monic irreducibles of degree . We must have . Consider multiple values of , e.g. and then to get a system of equations in . We can do this to show there are and irreducible polynomials of degree 1 and 2, respectively.

Answers: 6 and 99.

Exercise: Artin 16.M.5 (Frobenius automorphism, see Brian Bi)

6.6. Primitive Elements

Definition 43. Let . If there exists such that , say is a primitive element of the extension field .
Theorem 33 (Primitive Element Theorem). Every finite extension (finite degree) of a field of characteristic zero contains a primitive element.
Proof

Say where is a basis for as an -vector space. Proceed by induction on . There is nothing to prove when . By induction, it suffices to prove the theroem to the case of two elements: and .

It suffices to prove the following lemmas:

Lemma 35 (Artin 15.6.4d). The monic gcd of is the same whether computed in or .
Proof

[TODO]

□

Lemma 36 (Artin 15.8.2). Let be a field of characteristic zero, and let . For all but finitely many , is a primitive element for over .
Proof

Let and be the irreducible polynomials for and respectively over . Let be a field extension of in which and split completely. Call their roots and with and .

Since the characteristic is zero, the roots are are distinct (previous Lem). let . For , the equation holds for at most one . So for all but finitely many elements of , the will be distinct. We will show that if is not one of these “bad” values, then will be a primitive element.s

Let . To show that is primitive, it suffices to show that since that implies as well. Let . The coefficients are in but not . By construction, . If we show this is is the only common root, then it is an element of by Lemma 35, showing that is an element of .

Thus we need to show is not a root of when . This follows by choice of : .

□

□

Remark. The statement is true for finite fields but has a different proof. For infinite fields of characteristic , e.g. , we need an additional hypothesis. We won’t consider that case since we don’t study such fields.
